sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cardiogenic shock is a severe haemodynamic syndrome marked by inadequate cardiac output, resulting in persistent tissue hypoperfusion and a high risk of multiorgan failure and mortality. It is commonly associated with sustained hypotension and signs of end-organ hypoperfusion due to a primary cardiac cause such as acute myocardial infarction, myocarditis, or dysrhythmia.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Patient History and Risk Factors
- Patient in shock, all other causes ruled out, and identification of predisposing conditions such as acute myocardial infarction, cardiomyopathy, or prior cardiac events is essential
- Respiratory Findings
- Tachypnoea and/or hypoxia
- Pulmonary congestion
- Cardiovascular Manifestations
- Sustained hypotension
- Jugular venous distension
- Signs of Poor Perfusion
- Poor peripheral perfusion
- Oliguria
- Other Features
- Peripheral oedema
- Chest pain or discomfort

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Management should focus on oxygenation, ventilation, perfusion support, and rapid identification of the cardiac cause
- Monitor for arrhythmias, worsening hypotension, pulmonary oedema, and respiratory failure
- The risk of cardiogenic shock during or after AMI is higher in older adults, patients with diabetes, those with prior AMI, and those presenting with hypotension
- Avoid routine fluid loading. If hypotension may be partly preload-related, use only small cautious fluid challenges with frequent reassessment

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position the patient appropriately for breathing and perfusion
- Consider ventilatory support where indicated
- Titrate oxygen to clinical need
- Perform frequent vital signs and close haemodynamic monitoring
- Apply ECG monitoring and watch closely for arrhythmia
- Consider aspirin if ACS is suspected and there is no clear contraindication
- Monitor for rapid deterioration and consider other causes of shock

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG
- If due to arrhythmia, follow that guideline
- If due to ischaemia, follow that guideline
- Establish vascular access
- Assess volume status carefully; if hypovolaemia is not suspected, avoid routine fluid loading and use only small cautious fluid challenges when clinically indicated
- Apply defibrillation pads if deterioration risk is high
- Seek early senior advice regarding vasoactive or inotropic support in persistent hypotension
- Consider non-invasive ventilatory support where available and appropriate

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- A high percentage of this cohort of patients end up in cardiac arrest

**Cardiogenic Shock can be caused by:**
  - Impaired contractility
  - Myocardial ischemia and complications
  - Myocarditis
  - Myocardial contusion – Trauma
  - Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy
  - Septic shock
  - Dysrhythmia
  - Tachycardia
  - Bradycardia
  - Valvular dysfunction
  - Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HOCM)
